reason: The Maltese Falcon’s Sam Spade is the cynical PI archetype at the heart of ’40s noir.
The Big Sleep nails the smoky tension of a hard-boiled private investigator in ’46.
Casablanca’s Rick Blaine embodies the pessimistic, world-weary private eye in B&W.
The Third Man’s shadowy post-war streets and cross-cutting cynicism define classic noir.
Notorious fuses espionage and femme fatale in a refined ’46 black-and-white thriller.
To Catch a Thief’s cat-and-mouse luxe noir crafts a lethal romance in B&W.
Laura’s portrait-perfect mystery spins around a dangerous beauty and a jaded detective.
Sunset Blvd’s morbid introspection and film-noir style spotlight Hollywood’s dark underbelly.
Citizen Kane’s framing and fatalism laid the blueprint for cinematic noir dread.
A Grand Day Out’s minimalism nods to noir’s visual chiaroscuro even in animation.
